properties.field_incident_overview:
Reported: 4/22/26 at 6:36 pm

Location: Samuel R. McKelvie National Forest

Jurisdiction: Federal

Andrews Fire Afternoon Update 04-23-2026

The Andrews Fire has been declared 100% contained. Upon more accurating mapping it was determined that the fire is 570 acres. 

Crews will continue to patrol the fire's permieter and within it, doing mop-up and montioring over the next several days. 

The Red Flag Warning is still in effect until 8:00 PM CDT. 

Andrews Fire Morning Update 04-23-2026

The Andrews Fire was reported on April 22, 2026 at approximately 6:36 PM on the Samuel R. McKelvie National Forest. The current estimated burn area is 2500 acres and is being managed by Nebraska National Forests and Grasslands wildland fire crews. The fire has been burning in the north central area of the McKelvie National Forest in grass fuels.

Crews worked through the night with successful suppression efforts and are currently seeing minimal activity. Current strategy for today and the following days will be patrolling, mop-up, and monitoring the burned and active fire areas. Firefighters doing mop-up is a critical part of wildland fire efforts, firefighters extinguish hot spots to prevent reignition.

There are multiple active new wildfires adjacent to the McKelvie National Forest that US Forest Service wildland fire crews, along with local, state, and federal agencies are assisting with in the area.

There is currently a Red Flag Warning in effect through 8:00 PM today, April 23, 2026. Strong winds are forecast to continue throughout today.

Stage 1 Fire Prohibitions remain in place for Nebraska National Forests and Grasslands units.
properties.title: Andrews Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
The Hazen Fire transitioned to a Type 4 Organization on Friday, May 8 at 7am. Future updates will be made to this incident as notable activity occurs.

 

History:

The Hazen Fire was detected on Saturday, May 2 at approximately 2:50 pm. The fire is burning in salt cedar, grass and brush approximately 1 mile southwest of Buckeye, AZ. The fire is in the response area for the City of Buckeye Fire Department and AFMA. They provided initial response along with other departments to include Goodyear Fire Department, Surprise Fire-Medical Department and others.  

Arizona Department of Forestry and Fire Management (DFFM) assumed command of the fire the evening of May 2 at the request of City of Buckeye Fire Department and Arizona Fire & Medical Authority (AFMA). The incident was under the command of a DFFM Type 3 Incident Management Team. Official updates will be provided on social media accounts for DFFM. Updates are provided as they occur and are verified for accuracy.

properties.field_incident_overview:
The Jones Fire is 100% contained at 84 acres. All resources have been released and the incident was turned back over to the Wickenburg Fire Department for any further monitoring. All objectives have been met, including fence repair and dozer line rehabilitation. There is no heat remaining along the fire's edge and no visible smokes showing. 

On Friday, crews finished all chainsaw operations and continued line improvement and mop up along the southwest side of the fire. Today's work also included repairing dozer line within the river bottom to clean it up and restore the area to its natural state. 

Starting today, Stage 1 fire restrictions are in place within State Trust lands in Gila, Maricopa, and Pinal Counties. For fire restriction information: WildlandFire.az.gov. 

The US 60 is fully open. Drivers should use caution through the area as engines continue to work alongside the road. 

 

The Jones Fire was reported on May 11 burning approximately 1 mile southeast of Wickenburg, AZ.  Arizona Department of Forestry and Fire Management (DFFM) is in command of the fire. The fire is burning in the Hassayampa River bottom in salt cedar, cottonwood, grass and brush.

Forward progression was stopped at 6:35pm on May 11.  The fire is under full suppression.

Evacuation Shelter: Red Cross Arizona has opened a shelter for evacuees at Wickenburg High School Gym located 1090 S. Vulture Mine Road, Wickenburg, AZ 85390

Traffic Information: visit az511.com

properties.field_incident_description: Wildfire across three counties in Southwest Kansas. The counties of Morton, Meade, and Clark.
properties.field_incident_overview:
On May 14, 2026 a powerful storm system moved through southwest Kansas. Lightning strikes, fueled by strong winds and very dry vegetation, started multiple fires across the landscape. The fires quickly surpassed the local and mutual aid response. State assistance was requested. Aviation, KFS DFMOs, and in-state Wildfire Taskforces were mobilized. Many of the initial fires merged and are now being managed as a complex fire. Kansas Governor Kelly issued a State of Emergency Declaration on Friday, May 15, 2026. Local declarations have been issued; there is no federal emergency declaration. 

On Monday, May 18, the Meade County Fires are now under one Incident Command System and will be called the Meade Lake Complex. 

On May 20, 2026, the fire region received precipitation and higher humidity, which helped increase containment on both complexes. Non-local resources will be returned to their home units, and the fires will be monitored and patrolled by the local fire departments. The weather conditions in southwest Kansas have not dramatically changed. There is still potential for new starts to burn intensely and grow rapidly. 

The fires under the Meade Lake Complex are the Wolf Canyon Fire, the Meade County Complex 1 and 2, the Proffitt Lake fire, and the 2 unstaffed fires in the southern part of the county.  

Meade Lake Complex -  Jeff Latham and Troy Mueller - Incident Commanders      

  • 91,616 acres
  • 100% contained

The 500-Acre Pond Fire and the Herman Range Fire grew together on Sunday May, 17, 2026. All fires in Clark County will also be a complex of fires. The complex will include the Bouziden Range Fire and the Lexington Fire

Herman Range Complex - Chad Maybery Incident Commander

On Monday, May 18, 2026, the Herman Ranch Fire made a significant run to the south when the forecasted cold front impacted the fire’s open containment lines along the southern edge of the incident. The acres and map do not reflect that event. 

  • 36,194 acres
  • 90% Contained
properties.title: Southwest Kansas Wildfire Outbreak

properties.field_incident_overview:
The South Fork Fire ignited on June 9, 2026, in the Soldier Creek Wilderness area of the Nebraska National Forest’s Pine Ridge Ranger District near Crawford, Nebraska. Local responders, U.S. Forest Service crews and volunteer firefighting partners engaged the fire early as dry fuels, steep terrain and strong winds drove rapid spread. U.S. Highway 20 was closed by the Nebraska Department of Transportation as the fire jumped the highway. A red flag warning was in effect through Wednesday, June 10.

By June 10, the fire had grown to nearly 9,000 acres and Nebraska’s Governor Pillen officially issued an emergency declaration for the South Fork Fire. It was fueled by rugged terrain, dry vegetation including timber and grassy undergrowth, and strong winds. On June 11 at 6 am the Rocky Mountain Complex Incident Management Team 2, under the command of Jay Mickey, assumed control of the fire.

On June 12, containment increased to 7% even as the fire grew to about 28,067 acres. Resources including National Guard units, ground crews, dozers and aircraft focused on defending the towns of Crawford and the Fort Robinson State Park area. The Red Cross established community shelter options at the Chadron State College–Andrews Hall.

By June 15, containment was at 55% and the fire had grown to 38,914 acres. 581 personnel were supporting the fire. Forecasters predicted that the week ahead would bring sustained winds anticipated between 23 and 30 miles per hour, and the possibility of gusts escalating to as high as 50 miles per hour. 

Nebraska’s Governor, Jim Pillen received an operational visit of the South Fork Fire on June 16. He visited ICP and was provided with a comprehensive briefing. He had the opportunity to communicate with members of his constituency as well as incident team members. 

Containment reached 80% on June 17, with the acres climbing to 39,057. Although conditions continued to change, fire activity was generally quiet. A NIROPS flight (a thermal imaging aircraft used to map active wildfires) was ordered after several areas of hot spots were discovered. 

Line is now in place around the entire fire perimeter. Crews are working to secure and mop up the containment line and will continue for several days. Suppression repair work is also u begin underway and will continue in the next few days. 

On June 20th the overall acreage of the South Fork Fire increased to 39,696 due to more accurate satellite mapping showing previously unseen grassy areas and containment increased to 90%.

Rocky Mountain CIMT2 transfered command to the Nebraska Type 3 team on Sunday, June 21, 2026, at 6 p.m.  

Please follow the Nebraska National Forest on Facebook and the Nebraska National Forest website's Alerts and Notices page for further updates.
properties.title: South Fork Fire

properties.field_incident_description:
The Snake River Fire began on the evening of June 12th, 2026. The fire worked its way along Wawawai Road, approximately 4 miles west of Clarkston, in light flashy fuels and steep rocky terrain. Hand crews continued to gain access to the fire to cold trail, a method of controlling a partly extinguished fire edge by carefully inspecting and feeling with the hand for heat to detect any fire, digging out every live spot, and trenching any live edge, the perimeter of the fire throughout the day to increase containment. Crews worked on gridding 150 feet interior of the fireline around the entire perimeter of the fire extinguishing any heat they found. With the rugged terrain and unseasonably hot temperatures, crews had to navigate at a slower pace in order to safely work the fireline. Throughout the day state mobilization crews also assessed the structures threatened in the Moses community. With minimal fire activity throughout the day, two 20-person hand crews were reassigned to other nearby evolving incidents.

Northeast Washington Interagency Incident Management Team #1 has taken command of the fire as of 4:00PM on June 13th.

Evacuations and Closures: No evacuations are in effect at this time and there are no road closures

Weather: Temperature: High 91/Low 55. Mainly clear skies. Winds NE at 10 to 15 mph.
properties.field_incident_overview:
The Snake River Fire was reported at 5:55 PM on June 12th, along the Wawawai Road, approximately 4 miles west of Clarkston.  The fire quickly progressed north in flashy fuels (grasses and sagebrush) and in steep, rocky terrain, threatening rangeland, powerlines, communication infrastructure, rail infrastructure, and residences as Moses community.  Crews from Whitman County Fire Districts 14 and 12, Asotin County, DNR, and the nearby cities of Lewiston, Clarkson and Asotin engaged the fire, with the aid of a type 1 and type 2 helicopters.  State mobilization was authorized at 9:00PM on June 12th, coordinating additional district crews to respond to the fire. 

Northeast Washington Interagency Incident Management Team #1 has taken command of the fire as of 4:00PM on June 13th.

properties.field_incident_overview:
The Flat Fire is a lightning-caused wildfire located approximately 9 miles southwest of Heber, Arizona within Navajo Count. The fire is currently estimated at 134 acres and is 83% contained. The incident will transition on June 29th at 6:00am from the White Mountain Zone Type 3 team to a Type 4 organization.

During the current operational period, fire activity remained minimal, consisting primarily of smoldering and creeping within the interior. Firefighters will continue mop up operations and perimeter patrols to secure containment lines and keep the fire within the established perimeter. Fuels consist primarily of medium logging slash, brush, and tall grasses.

A Red Flag Warning remains in effect through Sunday evening, with dry breezy conditions and near-critical fire weather expected to continue into next week. Gusty winds may produce interior flare ups and localized spotting potential over the next 72 hours; however, no current threat to communities, infrastructure, or other values at risk has been identified.

There has been no reported damage to structures or infrastructure, no civilian impacts. Approximately 192 personnel remain assigned to the incident in support of suppression and containment efforts.

Operational priorities remained focused on patrolling and securing the fire perimeter, completing mop-up where needed, implementing suppression repair, and maintaining containment.
properties.title: Flat Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
The lightning-caused fire is located west of Darwin Ranch, near upper Gros Ventre Falls in the Gros Ventre Wilderness on the Jackson Ranger District of the Bridger-Teton National Forest, and is burning in heavy timber in steep challenging terrain. The fire was called 100% contained on Sunday, July 12. The fire still has some interior smokes that are in snag patches. Crews will continue to work on repair.

Fire managers are implementing direct extinguishment and indirect confinement strategies on the Kinky Creek Fire. A direct extinguishment strategy is when firefighters engage the active flame front directly to stop fire spread. An indirect confinement strategy is when firefighters work away from the active flame front to steer or contain the fire.

The forest has issued a trail closure order.

The fire danger rating is currently “high” for the Teton Interagency Dispatch area, which includes Bridger-Teton National Forest, Grand Teton National Park, and the National Elk Refuge.

For other news, events and information, visit the Bridger-Teton National Forest website and follow the Bridger-Teton on Facebook and X @BridgerTetonNF.
properties.title: Kinky Creek Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
The Cottonwood Fire started on June 22, 2026 and experienced rapid growth fueled by extremely dry fuels and weather conditions conducive to fire spread. 

Great Basin Complex Incident Management Team (CIMT) #4 took command of the Cottonwood Fire on Wednesday, July 8, 2026 at 6:00 am.  CIMT #4 will continue to support the fire operations and carry forward work with the Beaver and Paiute County Sheriffs’ Offices and other cooperating agencies, particularly in evaluating existing closures and evacuations for reduction and reentry when conditions are safe enough for residents and the public. 

Firefighters are working to minimize acres burned by constructing direct containment lines along the fire perimeter, and air resources continue to support firefighters on the ground with fire suppression objectives. Weather conditions, dry fuels, steep terrain, and limited access continue to be major factors as crews work to increase containment.

Live Air Quality and Smoke information is available on this page if you scroll down below the Incident Info section.
properties.title: Cottonwood Fire

properties.field_incident_description: The fire is burning in the west side of Olympic National Park on a steep, densely forested slope approximately 5 miles west-northwest of Mount Olympus.
properties.field_incident_overview:
Situation: The lightning-caused Mt. Tom Creek Fire is burning high on a remote mountain slope on the west side of Olympic National Park. The area is in steep, densely forested terrain approximately five miles west‑northwest of Mount Olympus. Backpackers first reported the fire on June 24th.

The fire is approximately six miles from the Hoh Rain Forest administrative site. The Hoh Rain Forest Visitor Center, campground, and trails all remain open to the public.

Firefighters are using indirect confinement tactics as part of a full-suppression strategy. These tactics include removing vegetation around recreation and administrative infrastructure and using helicopters to drop water to cool host spots and keep the fire within its current footprint. The fire is constrained by natural features on a couple flanks, where fire managers have determined it is 27% contained.

High relative humidity and periodic precipitation may constrain fire behavior, but are unlikely to extinguish the fire.

Smoke: Smoke may be visible from various locations on the Olympic Peninsula. Air quality can change quickly.  For current smoke information, visit: https://fire.airnow.gov

Fire Restrictions: Olympic National Park implemented fire restrictions June 25th. Dispersed and backcountry fires are prohibited. Fires within established metal fire rings in developed campgrounds and picnic areas are exempt from this prohibition. Propane and gas stoves are allowed, if operated at least three feet away from flammable vegetation and forest litter.

Air Space Restrictions: A Temporary Flight Restriction is in place. If you fly, we can’t. Every time a drone is spotted near the fire all aircraft are grounded until we can be sure the drone is clear of this area: http://knowbeforeyoufly.org
properties.title: Mount Tom Creek Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
The Wild Goose Fire began on June 26, 2026 just south of Wild Goose Canyon on the Fishlake National Forest. It is located east of Holden and south of Scipio, Utah. The fire burned in mixed fuel types and steep terrain. The fire grew very quickly in the first 24 hours, prompting evacuations of nearby communities and a closure order on Fishlake National Forest.

In the first days of the incident, cooler temperatures allowed firefighters to actively engage the fire, installing containment and contingency lines before increased temperatures and heavier winds created critical fire weather conditions. 

Fuels are exceptionally dry for this time of year and contributed to extreme fire behavior, but the lines created in the first week of GBT7's tenure have held and largely curtailed fire spread. Since July 3, fire behavior has remained moderate. On July 10, all evacuation orders were lifted and the Fishlake National Forest Closure was reduced in size to reopen popular recreation areas to the south of the fire.

On July 11 at 1900, command of the fire transitioned back to a local Type 4 Incident Commander on the Fishlake National Forest.

properties.field_incident_overview:
The Babylon Fire, began on Friday, June 26, 2026, about 25 miles southwest of Monticello, Utah. The fires continued significant growth is driven by a combination of low humidity, extremely dry vegetation and at times gusty winds across the region. Firefighters are actively working to contain the blaze using a mix of direct suppression and indirect confinement strategies. Over 1,000 personnel are assigned to the fire.

Due to active firefighting operations from the Babylon Fire, extensive public land closures are now in effect to ensure public and firefighter safety. All Forest Service lands, roads, and trails within the Monticello Ranger District of the Manti-La Sal National Forest are strictly closed, alongside the entire Needles District of Canyonlands National Park. Additionally, the Bureau of Land Management (BLM) has closed all managed lands west of Harts Draw Road, extending north through the Indian Creek Corridor to Indian Creek Falls, which encompasses Shay Mesa, Beef Basin, Dark Canyon, and Sweet Alice. For your safety and the safety of emergency crews, the public is advised to completely avoid the Monticello Ranger District and stay clear of all BLM and Forest Service lands south of Highway 211, west of Highway 191, and north of Highway 95.
properties.title: Babylon Fire

properties.field_incident_description:

Firefighters are working to protect life and property, support local communities, conduct structure protection where needed, and construct and improve containment lines around the fire.

Current suppression actions include direct and indirect line construction, line preparation, and aviation support as conditions allow. Hot and dry weather may contribute to active fire activity.
properties.field_incident_overview:
The Cherry Fire was reported at approximately 10:30 a.m. Friday, June 26, 2026, and grew rapidly under extreme fire weather. It was reported six miles southwest of Highway 36. The fire is burning primarily in grass, sagebrush, and scattered juniper. It made a significant run Friday, June 26th, extending approximately 11 miles from south to north in only a few hours. 

Great Basin Complex Incident Management Team 3, which was overseeing the nearby Iron Fire, took over command of the Cherry Fire on Saturday, June 27 at 6:00 a.m.

properties.field_incident_description: The Far Draw, Doe Canyon and Ferris Fires began on June 27, 2026. The three fires merged together and are all being referred to as the Ferris Fire. Low humidity, high temperatures and historically dry fuels have been causing extreme fire behavior.
properties.field_incident_overview:
The Ferris Fire is burning approximately 15 miles northwest of Dolores, Colorado on the San Juan National Forest, Bureau of Land Management, and private lands. Lightning was the cause. 

Strong winds, high heat, low humidity and extremely dry fuels drove fire behavior for the first two weeks before monsoonal moisture moved into Southwest Colorado. Now, as the fire nears containment, crews are working on suppression repair to leave the area in the best condition possible. This work involves repairing roads, naturalizing dozer line, chipping cut brush and trees, clearing culverts, and removing hose, pumps, and other equipment.

Evacuations and Closures:  All evacuations have been lifted. Click this link for an interactive Evacuations Status Map for real-time information for Dolores, Montezuma and San Miguel counties. You can also follow Dolores County Office of Emergency Management for additional details in that county, and San Miguel County Sheriff for that county. The San Juan National Forest has issued a Fire Area Closure. The Bureau of Land Management has an Emergency Land Closure in place as well.
properties.title: Ferris Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
DAILY UPDATES:  English (pdf)   |    Actualización del Incendio (pdf)    |  Operational Update (Video)  

MAPS:  Public Information   |   Progression

 

INITIAL SITUATION:  The Willow Fire located approximately 6 miles west of Leadville, CO, started on June 28, 2026. Southwest Area Team 4, led by Incident Commander, Andrew Mandell, assumed command at 7:00 a.m. on June 30, 2026.

 

CURRENT SITUATION: Very light rain fell primarily on the Bald Eagle Mountain side of the fire on Wednesday. Thunderstorms produced lightning, requiring firefighters to temporarily disengage from firefighting operations due to safety. Significant rainfall is needed to penetrate the forest canopy and increase fuel moisture. The fire continues to move toward sparse, high-elevation fuels in the Mount Massive Wilderness. Interior fire activity was observed, including isolated torching as unburned islands of vegetation continued to consume. Smoke is still visible due to smoldering near the Fish Hatchery and along the southern end of the fire area. Crews continue to mop up and search for hotspots to extinguish. Firefighters continue building a contingency line in the southwestern portion of the fire. Vegetation is still being chipped in the Halfmoon Creek area. Suppression repair efforts have begun across the fire area, except on primary containment lines. 
 

EVACUATIONS: For current closure and evacuation information, please follow Lake County Office of Emergency Management. We ask that the public respect the closures and evacuation area and avoid the area of Mt Massive and Hagerman Pass.

A FAA Temporary Flight Restriction (TFR) is in effect and any private aircraft or drone that violates the TFR could face serious criminal charges. For more information on drones the public can visit the FAA’s website at www.KnowBeforeYouFly.org “If you fly, we can’t!”

properties.field_incident_overview:
The Gold Mountain Fire was discovered on June 27, 2026, at approximately 5:33 pm 2 miles northeast of Ouray, CO. Fire initially started burning in mixed conifer vegetation located on inaccessible terrain above the east side of Highway 550. Local, State, and Federal initial attack fire resources were ordered immediately. The fire spread quickly running up steep slopes. A local Type 3 Incident Management Team was assigned on June 27, 2026. Aviation resources were ordered throughout the next day, but high winds and terrain features shut down the use of aviation resources. Fire became very active, with group torching and crowning, increasing the fire growth to the east by 2,000 acres. Fire resources provided public safety and worked to protect structures. Rocky Mountain Complex Incident Management Team 3 was ordered on June 28, 2026, and took command on June 29, 2026, at 6 pm.  Red Flag conditions over the following week led to extreme fire behavior increasing the fire growth to over 30,000 acres by July 6, 2026.  Rocky Mountain Complex Incident Management Team 2 assumed command July 14,2026 following a coordinated, planned transition.  

Firefighters are using a risk-based suppression strategy that prioritizes public and firefighter safety while protecting communities, critical infrastructure, water systems and access along the Highway 550 corridor. Crews continue to mop up and strengthen fire lines near Highway 550 and Cow Creek, secure the fire edge around Nate Creek, and construct and reinforce indirect line along the western perimeter toward Bureau of Land Management lands and south toward Silver Jack Reservoir. 

Where direct engagement is unsafe or impractical, firefighters are using natural features, indirect containment lines, point protection around homes and other values at risk, and pumps, hoses and large water tanks to support structure protection. Aerial resources continue to monitor and support operations along inaccessible portions of the fire.   Although 
thunderstorms may bring periods of rain, critically dry fuels, lightning and erratic outflow winds remain concerns.
properties.title: Gold Mountain Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
Background: On the afternoon of Sunday, June 21, the Mogollon Baldy lookout detected a lightning-caused fire on Sacaton Mountain (elevation 10,658 feet) in the Gila Wilderness. The fire, which is on the Glenwood Ranger District, was initially about 4.5 miles west of the lookout tower. 

Firefighters quickly conducted an aerial assessment and estimated the fire—burning in brush, timber, logs, and snags—was about 2 acres and exhibiting moderate fire behavior. By late evening, the lookout reported the fire had grown to approximately 4 acres.

The fire’s location presents extreme risks to both ground and aerial firefighters: It is burning among numerous snags, a dense tangle of fallen trees, and thick brush regeneration from the 2012 Whitewater-Baldy Fire. The landscape is very rugged and steep (e.g., 40–60 percent slopes). Safety zones and escape routes are nonexistent. Emergency extraction times would be unacceptably long (i.e., 6+ hours) and logistically difficult. High-elevation flying, especially during hot weather, is very hazardous and an unacceptable transfer of risk to the pilot.

Smoke & Air Quality: Smoke is visible from numerous areas in and surrounding the Gila National Forest. Scroll down for air quality information and the AirNow Fire and Smoke Map.

The fire was wind driven by 40+ mph winds with gusts estimated to be up to 100 mph. It has impacted the community of Beulah, Colorado and the surrounding area in both Custer and Pueblo Counties.

The fire is currently estimated at 9,735 acres with 75% containment. Fire activity remains active, exhibiting primarily backing and smoldering behavior. Fuels involved continue to include grass, brush, timber, and agricultural crops.

Firefighter safety remains the highest priority. Wind-driven fire runs, moderate temperatures, and environmental hazards continue to present operational challenges. Crews will continue to construct, improve, and cold trail control lines to prevent further fire spread. Resources will also focus on structure protection and supporting containment efforts within assigned divisions while minimizing damage to both public and private property.

Suppression efforts focused on mop-up operations to strengthen containment, secure the fire perimeter, and reduce the potential for flare-ups. Firefighters will continue to patrol and monitor the fire area as red flag fire weather conditions continue. Fire personnel will be repairing control lines where safe to do so around the perimeter of the fire. Crews will be focused on securing the fire lines ahead of the expected gusty winds and monitoring them as they are wind tested.

properties.field_incident_description:
Last Updated July 24, 7:00 PM

Non-Boundary Waters Canoe Area Wilderness Wildfires
Camp: Approximately 4,403 acre.23% Contained. Location–5 miles north of Winton. Handcrews continued to build direct handline along the entire southern perimeter of the fire, connecting to Newton Lake on the east and other natural features on the west. Crews will continue to build and improve direct and indirect lines and structure protection along Fernberg Road, identifying structures and potential for contingency lines to the south and east out to Snowbank.

Sioux: Approximately 12,489 acres. 27% Contained. Location–North of the Echo Trail and east of Jeanette Campground. Portions of the fire are burning in BWCAW. Handcrews continue to work on establishing direct handline on the southern perimeter of the fire that crossed Echo Trail including digging handline and installing hoselays and a sprinkler system. This work with handcrews will continue today. Firefighters continue working further south from the immediate fire perimeter to develop a plan for additional indirect containment lines.

Boundary Waters Canoe Area Wilderness Wildfires

Little Knife: Originated in Ontario and crossed into the United States. The approximate fire acreage in the United States has been reduced to 4,958 acres due to more accurate mapping.13% containment. Fire activity is currently minimal. Multiple reconnaissance flights have been conducted and a Chinook aircraft was used to strategically drop water on hot spots. Managed by the National Incident Management Organization (NIMO).

Bear Trap (Bear Trap and Dark Fires combined): Approximately 37,837 total acres (29,739 acres in the United States). 38% Contained. Location–South of Lac La Croix, 20 miles north of Ely. Structure protection with hose lays and sprinklers continued around the northern edge of Big Lake in Whispering Pines and Big Lake Lodge as well as private properties on the south side of the lake. Today, structure protection will continue around Big Lake as well as crews and equipment will begin clearing and widening the Echo Trail to the east to connect the Echo Trail to the Camp Fire and to the west towards the Sioux Fire.

Thumb: 17,199 total acres (16,853 acres in the United States). 56% Contained. Remote and located 6 miles south of Gakijiwanong Anishnaabe Nation Canada. The majority of fire activity was along the southwestern perimeter of the fire area, where the fire is backing down into natural barriers, close to the northern perimeter of the Sioux Fire. Aviation resources will monitor the fire during today’s operations

Wildfires West of Echo Lake and East of U.S. Hwy 53

The Chub Fire is the most active fire in this area. All the fires have firefighters and engines working on a variety of suppression tactics including building line, mopping up and patrolling and monitoring. There is a combined containment of 85% on these eleven fires.

Chub: 344.6 acres. 86% contained. Location – North of Chub Lake, southwest of Crane Lake. Crews will secure dozer line and handlines, followed by mop up and gridding to extinguish pockets of heat throughout the fire’s interior. 

493: 1.9 acre.0% Contained. Location – South of Long Lake. Crews will secure dozer line and handlines, followed by mop up and gridding to extinguish pockets of heat throughout the fire’s interior.

Echo 2, Beaver Tail, Ridge, Creek, Marion Creek Trail, Nine, Camp 5/Duck, Bug Creek, Coffin Fires: 62.5 acres combined for all nine fires. All are 100% contained. Crews continue to patrol these fires, searching for smoke and hot spots and extinguishing any lingering heat.

properties.field_incident_overview:
A lightning storm moved through the Tri-Forest area on the evening of July 7, 2026, igniting multiple fires. The Salmon Fire was discovered shortly after midnight, and the Olive Butte Fire was discovered that morning on July 8, 2026. Initial attack resources responded promptly but were spread thin due to a number of other starts in the area. NW Team 12 took command of the Salmon and Olive Butte fires along with five smaller fires on July 11 at 6 AM. The five small fires were contained by the evening of July 11, but two larger fires remain burning in very steep terrain with heavy dead and downed trees that are drier than normal for this time of year and readily consuming. This heavy fuel loading also has slowed line construction efforts, coupled with running fire and long-range spotting creating a very dynamic and rapidly changing fire environment.

Evacuation notices for the communities of Greenhorn, Olive Creek, and Granite of Baker and Grant Counties are in effect. From July 12-17, the Oregon State Fire Marshal’s Green Incident Management Team, along with four structural task forces, supported structure protection operations.

Priorities remain public and firefighter safety, homes and cabins, private lands, and critical infrastructure. Firefighters are working the fires as directly as possible to protect these values at risk, using both ground resources and multiple helicopters and fixed wing aircraft dropping water and retardant.

The fire has also spread into the North Fork John Day Wilderness with additional natural resource values at risk.

properties.field_incident_overview:
The Claremont Fire was reported on July 6, 2026, approximately one-mile northeast of Boise in the Boise foothills. The fire is burning in the 8th Street Fire (1996) scar. The fuels include a mixture of short grass, brush and ponderosa pine regeneration. 

The fire is being managed under a full suppression strategy to protect lives, homes, critical infrastructure, recreation areas, and natural resources in the Boise foothills and surrounding communities. In addition to its proximity to development and recreational areas as well as steep terrain, a portion of the fire area contains unexploded military ordnances (UXO). 

Due to the location and complexity of the incident, the Eastern Area Team 1 was ordered to coordinate suppression efforts. On July 20th, the team transitioned with Great Basin Complex Incident Management Team 3 to continue management of the fire. 

Additional sources of information: 

  • USWFS Idaho - Great Basin (Facebook page)
  • Boise County Sheriff's Office (Facebook page)

  • Boise National Forest Alerts (Website)
properties.title: Claremont Fire

properties.field_incident_description: Full Suppression Wildfire
properties.field_incident_overview:
The Powder River Fire is burning approximately five miles west of Richland, Oregon. Resources have established an anchor point and are working across all sides of the fire using direct and indirect tactics where it is safe to do so. 

Fire Restrictions: The Bureau of Land Management has issued a Notice of Temporary Restriction Order for the Vale District: https://www.blm.gov/sites/default/files/docs/2026-07/orwa-fpo-val-070626.pdf

Smoke: Smoke from the Powder River Fire and other surrounding fires, continues to affect portions of eastern Oregon and neighboring areas. Individuals, especially those with respiratory conditions, should monitor local air quality reports and limit outdoor activities when smoke concentrations are high. For more information: https://outlooks.airfire.org/outlook/ad8568e1

Evacuations: Evacuation orders remain in effect in portions of Baker County. The public is urged to follow directions from the Baker County Sheriff's Office and remain aware of changing fire conditions. Fire managers understand the disruption these evacuations create and appreciate the public's cooperation as crews work to protect communities. For more information go to state RAPTOR map at: https://arcg.is/0iKuO91
properties.title: Powder River Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
The Little Giant Fire, started by lightning on July 15, is located 30 miles NW of Leavenworth, WA. It is in the Chiwawa River drainage on the Wenatchee River Ranger District of the Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest. It was originally estimated at 5 acres. Rappelers were dispatched for initial attack, and a 21-person initial attack crew arrived on July 17. Minimal aerial support was available to support firefighters on the ground.

Due to winds and increased fire behavior, as well as poor access in steep, inaccessible country, all firefighters disengaged and hiked out on July 18. Currently, a 20-person crew is working to brush the Chiwawa River Rd, FS 6200 and is available to assist with tactics to suppress the fire as it is safe for firefighters. Warm weather with low humidity has led to more active fire behavior since July 20th. Due to the inversion lifting and warmer temperatures on Wednesday, fire behavior was more active on the SE flank. Wednesday night through Thursday night the fire experienced extreme growth to the SE and E, impacting both Rock Creek drainage to basalt ridge and down towards Finner creek to the SE. The main fire spread south to Y creek due to a wind event. The fire is currently estimated at 7,558 acres, as of Thursday evening, July 23nd.

Chelan County Sheriff's Office issued a Level 1 Fire Advisory for the Trinity Area on Tuesday, July 21. Updates on alerts and evacuations can be obtained here on the Chelan County Emergency Management Facebook page, or the website at https://www.co.chelan.wa.us/emergency-management/pages/active-emergencies.
properties.title: Little Giant Fire

properties.field_incident_overview:
A lightning storm moved through Eastern Oregon on July 15, 2026, igniting multiple fires. The Egypt Fire was discovered on the morning of July 16, 2026. Initial attack resources from the Burns Interagency Fire Zone responded promptly but were spread thin due to several other starts in the area. Southern Area Team 2 took command of the Egypt Fire on July 19 at 6 AM. 

Priorities remain public and firefighter safety, private lands, and critical infrastructure. Firefighters are working the fire as directly as possible to protect these values at risk, using both ground resources and multiple helicopters and fixed wing aircraft.

properties.field_incident_description: Wildland Fire - Remains Under Investigation
properties.field_incident_overview:
Links - Daily Update (pdf)     |     Daily Informational Map (pdf)     |     Coming Soon - Daily Operations Update (video) 

 

The Biscar Fire started on July 18, 2026 within Lassen County.  It grew quickly with warm temperatures and strong winds pushing it eastward across Highway 395.  It is currently mapped at 69,344 acres with 42% containment.

Fire Activity: Firefighters have established good containment lines along the western flank, from Vincent Springs to Secret Creek and to Rye Ranch Springs. Crews continue to strengthen this line and mop up hot spots.  On the southern side, firefighters are making solid progress building fire lines near Rush Creek. Along Smoke Creek to Wilcox Canyon (eastern flank) crews are actively constructing direct containment lines.

Fire Weather: Weather conditions have remained favorable for firefighting efforts, but a shift is on the way. Conditions are expected to change today, with hot, very dry, and increasingly windy weather building Friday and Saturday. Firefighters are preparing for these conditions, which could bring increased fire activity later in the week.

Why the strange name?  Fires are often named after geographic features near the fire, and in this case, Biscar Reservoir is located just a few miles south of where the fire was first reported.  Pierre “Pete” Biscar (1881–1947),  was a French-born rancher who settled in Secret Valley, California, and constructed the first reservoir at the mouth of Snowstorm Canyon.

Incident Objectives:

  • Provide safety for first responders and the public through risk analysis and hazard mitigation.
  • Minimize acres burned utilizing direct extinguishment tactics.
  • Utilize resources where they can be most successful and ensure tactics have a high probability of success.
  • Protect and minimize impacts to communities, private property, infrastructure, cultural, natural resources and Sage Grouse habitat.
  • Strengthen collaboration and relationships with cooperators, ranchers, tribal communities, and the public by delivering accurate information while seeking opportunities to build new relationships.

Weather Advisory:  A Fire Weather Watch and Red Flag Warnings are in effect from Friday afternoon through Saturday evening for gusty winds and low humidity for northeast California and far northwest Nevada... The National Weather Service in Reno has issued a Fire Weather Watch for gusty winds and low humidity, which is in effect from Friday afternoon through Saturday evening. * 

  • Affected Area...Fire Weather Zone 270 - Surprise Valley California, Fire Weather Zone 278 - Eastern Lassen County, Fire  Weather Zone 458 - Northern Washoe County. 
  • Winds...Southwest 10 to 20 mph with gusts up to 35 mph. 
  •  Humidity...Afternoon minimums 6 to 9 percent. 
  • Duration...3 to 8 hours. 
  • Impacts...The combination of gusty winds and low humidity can cause fire to rapidly grow in size and intensity before first responders can contain them.

California  

  • Evacuation Orders: Orders have been issued for the following areas -  LAS-553.  An evacuation order is issued when there is an immediate threat to life. This is a lawful order to LEAVE NOW. The area is lawfully closed to public access.
  • Evacuation Warnings: Warnings have been issued for the following areas - LAS-543, LAS-545, and LAS-555  An evacuation order means there is a potential threat to life and/or property. Those who require additional time to evacuate, and those with pets and livestock should leave now.
  • Interactive Evacuation Map is available through https://protect.genasys.com/ 

For shelter information call (530) 254-4500: or visit the Lassen County Sheriff's Office Facebook: https://www.facebook.com/LassenSheriff/ 

Nevada

  • Level 2 (Set), and Level 3 (Go Now) Evacuation recommendations have been issued for portions of central Washoe County.  
  • Interactive Evacuation Map is available through https://experience.arcgis.com/experience/46cb79ef933d4485b9966a9730e2d944/page/Perimeter/ 

Please note that California and Nevada use different, though similar, programs.  Evacuation status information for each state is displayed within their respective programs.  

Area Closures: The Bureau of Land Management has instituted a temporary closure of public lands for the Biscar Fire.  Public lands near Snowstorm Road and Vincent Canyon, northeast of Susanville in Lassen County, are closed for wildland fire fighting operations.  Pursuant to 43 CFR 8364.1, this temporary closure is being implemented for public and firefighter safety during emergency fire suppression actions.  View the Full Closure Order and Map.

Fire Restrictions: The Bureau of Land Management issued seasonal fire restrictions effective July 15th for BLM-managed public lands within Lassen, Modoc, Plumas, Sierra, Siskiyou and Shasta counties in California and Washoe County, Nevada. These seasonal restrictions are in addition to the year-round, statewide fire prevention order. These fire orders for Applegate and Eagle Lake field offices are in place to help prevent wildfires and will remain in effect until further notice.

The fire restrictions prohibit campfires and barbecues except within established campfire rings and barbecue grills in certain designated recreation sites:   Read more at https://www.blm.gov/announcement/blm-announces-seasonal-fire-restrictions-ne-california-nw-nevada-0 

Highway Closures: Highway 395 has reopened with no traffic restrictions.       Please use caution when traveling in the area as firefighters may be working near the roads, and anticipate an increased amount of heavy equipment between the fire area and Susanville.  https://roads.dot.ca.gov/?roadnumber=395 

Smoke Impacts:  Please visit https://fire.airnow.gov/?lang=en#7.34/40.599/-119.644 for smoke information from the Biscar Fire.    Fore information about the impacts of smoke, and ways to protect your health, please visit https://www.airnow.gov/air-quality-and-health/fires-and-your-health/ 

About the Bureau of Land Management - Northern California District.  The BLM’s Northern California District manages about 3.5 million acres of public lands, covering a diverse landscape, ranging from the arid and rugged Great Basin to the fog shrouded forests of the North Coast. The Redding-based district office oversees field offices in Alturas, Arcata, Redding and Susanville and a field station in Cedarville. The district contains two distinct regions. On the east, the Applegate and Eagle Lake field offices oversee vast expanses of public lands in a high desert landscape, including about a million and a half acres in far western Nevada. Small communities depend on public lands for economic benefits, primarily through livestock grazing, and tourism uses ranging from hunting to mountain biking. These lands provide habitat for 14 wild horse herds and special status species, such as greater sage grouse. On the west, the Arcata and Redding field offices manage smaller, scattered blocks of public land with more emphasis on recreation demands from urban areas.

properties.title: Box Springs

properties.field_incident_overview:
The Rock Fire started in the Aberdeen area, south of Big Pine, north of Independence at approximately 4:50 p.m. on Monday, July 21. The cause is under investigation.

On Monday, the fire moved north towards Aberdeen, before shifting winds pushed it south. Crews then worked along the Black Rock Road to protect 8 Mile Ranch. Erratic and shifting winds near the fire have been challenging to firefighters. 

On the Inyo National Forest, the fire is currently impacting the Sawmill Trailhead near Division Creek Road. Three other trailheads are being impacted - Taboose, Red Lake and Birch. 

The Rock Fire is burning in grass and brush. Firefighters are working to keep the fire north of Division Creek Road. The fire is currently 11,635 acres and is 18% contained. 

The Rock Fire is in Unified Command  with CAL FIRE San Bernardino Unit, and the Inyo National Forest.

Information on evacuations, road closures, and shelters may be found at the Ready Inyo  Office of Emergency Management page.
properties.title: Rock Fire

properties.field_incident_overview: The Keystone Fire was ignited by lightning in the 2018 burn scar of the Indian Valley Fire on July 21, 2026. Local, state and federal ground and aerial firefighting resources responded and worked together to put retardant and water on the fire as quickly as possible. By evening, the Northwest Colorado Fire Management Unit placed an order for a Type 3 Incident Management Team, and Team Colorado assumed command of the fire at 6:00 a.m. July 23. There are currently no human lives or structures threatened.
properties.title: Keystone Fire

properties.field_incident_overview: Following lightning storms on July 17, Border 1 Fire and Border 2 Fire were discovered within North Cascades National Park Service Complex along the U.S./Canada border, east of Ross Lake, in steep, rocky, and remote terrain. Fire activity increased due to elevated fire weather conditions and Border 2 Fire combined on July 21, now known as the Border 2 Fire. The fire has crossed the international border into Canada and east onto Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest lands. The lightning-caused Border 2 Fire is being managed under a full-suppression strategy.
properties.title: Border 2 Fire

properties.field_incident_overview: The lightning-caused Luna Fire within North Cascades National Park Service Complex is located in a rock scree southwest of Luna Camp along Big Beaver Trail in steep, rough, and remote terrain.
properties.title: Luna Fire
